text: Last class you compared the old way and the new way of a process.
Today we look forward: timelines, forecasts, and a strategic plan.
We are going to lock the baseline in Q1.

I have finished the outlook for next year. Our projection shows demand will rise about fifteen percent.

Hi Fabiana, it is Klaus again. Before our planning call, here is the outlook for next year. Our projection is that demand will rise about fifteen percent, mostly in the second and third quarter. So the roadmap has three big initiatives. First, by the end of Q2 we will have rebuilt the supplier pipeline, and we anticipate two main headwinds: longer lead times and a tighter budget. Second, this time next year we will be running a fully scalable forecasting model, so the team can test different scenarios in minutes. Third, we are presenting the new timeline to the board on the tenth, and each deliverable on it has a clear owner and a date. I want you to set the baseline now, because once we launch, we will measure every target against it. Our trajectory is good, but nothing is automatic. So in our call, walk me through your forecast, your timeline, and the one scenario that worries you most. Talk soon.

Hi team, here is a quick look at where we are heading next year. Let me start with the outlook. Our projection shows steady growth, and the trajectory of the last two quarters supports it. So here is the roadmap. In the first quarter we are going to lock the baseline numbers, because every target depends on a solid starting point. By the middle of the year, we will have launched the new forecasting tool, and by December we will be tracking each deliverable against the timeline in real time. I want to be honest about the headwinds. Lead times are still long, and one scenario could push our biggest initiative back a quarter. That is why the plan is scalable. If demand rises sharply, the pipeline can absorb it without a full redesign. My ask is simple. Look at your part of the timeline, set your own baseline this week, and tell me which deliverable you anticipate missing, so we can plan for it now. We are not just predicting the future. We are deciding how to meet it.

First, the team sets the baseline, because every target needs a clear starting point. Then they study the outlook and build a projection for the next four quarters. Next, they draw the roadmap, with one owner and one date for every deliverable. After that, they map the main scenarios, so they can anticipate the biggest headwinds early. Finally, by the end of the year, they will have launched the plan, and they will be tracking each target against the timeline in real time.
Our projection shows demand will rise next quarter.
